perturbation.original_text: 1. Regardless of the form, format, or media on or in which the Confidential Information is provided and regardless of whether any such Confidential Information is marked as such, “Confidential Information” means (1) any and all information provided by or made available by the State to the Contractor in connection with the Contract and (2) any and all personally identifiable information (PII) (including but not limited to personal information as defined in Md. Ann. Code, General Provisions §4- 101(h) and Md. Ann. Code, State Govt. § 10- 1301(c)) and protected health information (PHI) as defined in 45 CFR 160.103 that is provided by a person or entity to the Contractor in connection with this Contrac t. Confidential Information includes, by way of example only, information that the Contractor views, takes notes from, copies (if the State agrees in writing to permit copying), possesses or is otherwise provided access to and use of by the State in relati on to the Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Regardless of the form, format, or media on or in which the Confidential Information is provided and regardless of whether any such Confidential Information is marked as such, “Confidential Information” means (1) any and all information provided by or made available by the State to the Contractor in connection with the Contract and (2) only that personally identifiable information (PII) which the State designates in writing as 'Confidential PII', notwithstanding the definitions in Md. Ann. Code, General Provisions §4- 101(h) and Md. Ann. Code, State Govt. § 10- 1301(c)) and protected health information (PHI) as defined in 45 CFR 160.103 that is provided by a person or entity to the Contractor in connection with this Contrac t. Confidential Information includes, by way of example only, information that the Contractor views, takes notes from, copies (if the State agrees in writing to permit copying), possesses or is otherwise provided access to and use of by the State in relati on to the Contract.
perturbation.explanation: This change narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' regarding PII, making it dependent on the State's designation rather than the statutory definition. This creates a contradiction because the state law defines PII regardless of the State's designation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text limits the scope of 'Confidential Information' by requiring the State to designate PII as 'Confidential PII'. This contradicts Md. Ann. Code, General Provisions § 4-101(h), which defines 'Personal Information' (a component of PII) based on its inherent nature, irrespective of whether the State formally designates it as such. The law does not allow for such a restrictive interpretation based on the State's designation.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the provided explanation of Maryland law regarding Personally Identifiable Information (PII). The original text states that 'Confidential Information' includes all PII as defined by Maryland law. The changed text narrows this definition, stating that only PII designated in writing as 'Confidential PII' by the State is considered confidential, despite the definitions in Md. Ann. Code. This directly contradicts the statutory definition of PII, as the State cannot arbitrarily redefine what constitutes PII, creating a misalignment with the law. The altered text undermines the legal obligation by allowing the state to selectively designate PII as confidential, ignoring the broader definition established by law.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The Contractor shall not, without the State’s prior written consent, copy, disclose, publish, release, transfer, disseminate, use, or allow access for any purpose or in any form, any Confidential Information except for the sole and exclusive purpose of performing under the Contract. The Contractor shall limit access to the Confidential Information to the Contractor’s Personnel who have a demonstrable need to know such Confidential Information in order to perform under the Contract an d who are bound by confidentiality obligations at least as restr ictive as those set forth in this Agreement. Contractor shall provide copies of such agreements to the State. The names of such personnel are attached hereto and made a part hereof as Attach ment I-2. Contractor shall update Attachment I-2 by adding additional names (whether Contractor’s Personnel or a subcontractor’s personnel) as needed, from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The Contractor shall not, without the State’s prior written consent, copy, disclose, publish, release, transfer, disseminate, use, or allow access for any purpose or in any form, any Confidential Information except for the sole and exclusive purpose of performing under the Contract. The Contractor shall limit access to the Confidential Information to the Contractor’s Personnel who have a demonstrable need to know such Confidential Information in order to perform under the Contract and who are bound by confidentiality obligations that they deem reasonable. Contractor shall provide copies of such agreements to the State. The names of such personnel are attached hereto and made a part hereof as Attach ment I-2. Contractor shall update Attachment I-2 by adding additional names (whether Contractor’s Personnel or a subcontractor’s personnel) as needed, from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: The change replaces 'at least as restrictive as those set forth in this Agreement' with 'that they deem reasonable', giving the Contractor's Personnel discretion over confidentiality obligations. This contradicts the intent of the contract and potentially violates HIPAA if PHI is involved, as the standard is no longer aligned with the contract's protective measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification, by allowing the Contractor's personnel to determine the reasonableness of confidentiality obligations, potentially weakens the protection of Protected Health Information (PHI) under HIPAA. 45 CFR § 164.502(e)(1)(ii) requires covered entities to obtain 'satisfactory assurances' from business associates that they will appropriately safeguard PHI. Allowing for discretion conflicts with this standard of 'satisfactory assurances'.

All covered entities, except "small health plans," must have been compliant with the Privacy Rule by April 14, 2003. 90 Small health plans, however, had until April 14, 2004 to comply. Small Health Plans. A health plan with annual receipts of not more than $5 million is a small health plan. 91 Health plans that file certain federal tax returns and report receipts on those returns should use the guidance provided by the Small Business Administration at 13 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 121.104 to calculate annual receipts. Health plans that do not report receipts to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), for example, group health plans regulated by the Employee Retirement Income Security Act 1974 (ERISA) that are exempt from filing income tax returns, should use proxy measures to determine their annual receipts. 92 See What constitutes a small health plan? Copies of the Rule & Related Materials See our Combined Regulation Text of All Rules section of our site for the full suite of HIPAA Administrative Simplification Regulations and Understanding HIPAA for additional guidance material. perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a requirement for confidentiality obligations to be 'at least as restrictive as those set forth in this Agreement' with a standard of 'that they deem reasonable'. This weakens the contractual protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with HIPAA requirements for 'satisfactory assurances' from business associates regarding the safeguarding of PHI, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states it is from the eCFR and is authoritative. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that if PHI is involved, this may violate HIPAA.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The Contr actor shall promptly advise the State in writing if it learns of any unauthorized use, misappropriation, or disclosure of the Confidential Information by any of the Contractor’s Personnel or the Contractor’s former Personnel. Contractor shall, at its own e xpense, cooperate with the State in seeking injunctive or other equitable relief against any such person(s).
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The Contr actor shall promptly advise the State in writing if it learns of any grossly negligent unauthorized use, misappropriation, or disclosure of the Confidential Information by any of the Contractor’s Personnel or the Contractor’s former Personnel. Contractor shall, at its own expense, cooperate with the State in seeking injunctive or other equitable relief against any such person(s).
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'grossly negligent', the contractor is only obligated to report uses that meet that standard, potentially failing to report less severe but still prohibited actions. This contradicts mandatory reporting requirements under data breach notification laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text limits the reporting obligation to 'grossly negligent' unauthorized uses or disclosures. This contradicts Md. Code, Commercial Law, § 14-3504, which requires notification of a data breach involving personal information without specifying a threshold of negligence. The law mandates notification regardless of the level of negligence involved, making the contract less stringent than state law.

(a) In this section: (1) “Breach of the security of a system” means the unauthorized acquisition of computerized data that compromises the security, confidentiality, or integrity of the personal information maintained by a business; and (2) “Breach of the security of a system” does not include the good faith acquisition of personal information by an employee or agent of a business for the purposes of the business, provided that the personal information is not used or subject to further unauthorized disclosure. (b) (1) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains computerized data that includes personal information of an individual residing in the State, when it discovers or is notified that it incurred a breach of the security of a system, shall conduct in good faith a reasonable and prompt investigation to determine the likelihood that personal information of the individual has been or will be misused as a result of the breach. (2) Subject to subsection (c)(4) of this section, unless the business reasonably determines that the breach of the security of the system does not create a likelihood that personal information has been or will be misused, the owner or licensee of the computerized data shall notify the individual of the breach. (3) Except as provided in subsection (d) of this section, the notification required under paragraph (2) of this subsection shall be given as soon as reasonably practicable, but not later than 45 days after the business discovers or is notified of the breach of the security of a system. (4) If after the investigation required under paragraph (1) of this subsection is concluded, the business determines that notification under paragraph (2) of this subsection is not required, the business shall maintain records that reflect its determination for 3 years after the determination is made. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces the term 'grossly negligent' before 'unauthorized use, misappropriation, or disclosure,' limiting the contractor's reporting obligation. This contradicts Md. Code, Commercial Law, § 14-3504, as shown in both scraped snippets, which requires notification of a data breach without specifying a threshold of negligence. The law mandates notification regardless of the level of negligence involved. Therefore, the modified contract is less stringent than the state law and creates a clear legal contradiction, because it could allow breaches that are not 'grossly negligent' to go unreported, which would violate the statute. The addition of 'grossly negligent' weakens the legal requirement for prompt investigation and notification of data breaches, as stated in snippet 1 (b)(1): 'A business... when it discovers or is notified that it incurred a breach of the security of a system, shall conduct in good faith a reasonable and prompt investigation...' and (b)(2): '...the owner or licensee of the computerized data shall notify the individual of the breach.' The modified text limits that obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 9. The parties further agree that: a. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Maryland; b. The rights and obligations of the Contractor under this Agreement may not be assigned or delegated, by operation of law or otherw ise, without the prior written consent of the State; c. The State makes no representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information; d. The invalidity or unenforceability of any provision of this Agreement shall not affect the validity or enforceability of any other provision of this Agreement; Attac hment I – Non-Disclosure Agreement (Contractor) Page I-3 effective date: April 17, 2018 e. Signatures exchanged by facsimile are effective for all purposes hereunder to the same extent as original signatures; f. The Recitals are not merely prefatory but are an int egral part hereof; and g. The effective date of this Agreement shall be the same as the effective date of the Agreement entered into by the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The parties further agree that: a. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Maryland, excluding its conflict of laws principles; b. The rights and obligations of the Contractor under this Agreement may not be assigned or delegated, by operation of law or otherw ise, without the prior written consent of the State; c. The State makes no representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information; d. The invalidity or unenforceability of any provision of this Agreement shall not affect the validity or enforceability of any other provision of this Agreement, unless such provision is deemed fundamental to the purpose of this agreement; Attac hment I – Non-Disclosure Agreement (Contractor) Page I-3 effective date: April 17, 2018 e. Signatures exchanged by facsimile are effective for all purposes hereunder to the same extent as original signatures; f. The Recitals are not merely prefatory but are an int egral part hereof; and g. The effective date of this Agreement shall be the same as the effective date of the Agreement entered into by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'excluding its conflict of laws principles' can lead to unintended application of other states' laws if Maryland's conflict of laws rules would have pointed to another jurisdiction. Introducing 'unless such provision is deemed fundamental to the purpose of this agreement' creates ambiguity as to which provisions are fundamental and could render the severability clause useless for important aspects of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland case law regarding conflict of laws principles and severability clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: By excluding the application of Maryland's conflict of laws principles, the contract attempts to override the established legal framework that Maryland courts use to determine which jurisdiction's law should apply when there are conflicts between different states' laws. Similarly, the amendment concerning fundamental provisions may affect the court's analysis in determining if the contract is valid. The changes undermine established principles of contract interpretation under Maryland law.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 1 / CHAPTER 1 / § 1 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 1 USC 1 : Words denoting number, gender, and so forth Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 1-GENERAL PROVISIONS CHAPTER 1-RULES OF CONSTRUCTION Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Short Title §1. Words denoting number, gender, and so forth In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words "insane" and "insane person" shall include every idiot, insane person, and person non compos mentis; the words "person" and "whoever" include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals; "officer" includes any person authorized by law to perform the duties of the office; "signature" or "subscription" includes a mark when the person making the same intended it as such; "oath" includes affirmation, and "sworn" includes affirmed; "writing" includes printing and typewriting and reproductions of visual symbols by photographing, multigraphing, mimeographing, manifolding, or otherwise. ( July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 ; June 25, 1948, ch. 645, §6, 62 Stat. 859 ; Oct. 31, 1951, ch. 655, §1, 65 Stat. 710 ; Pub. L. 112–231, §2(a), Dec. 28, 2012, 126 Stat. 1619 .) perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity and attempts to override established legal frameworks, potentially leading to a clear non-compliance. By excluding Maryland's conflict of laws principles and adding a clause about 'fundamental' provisions, the changed text weakens the contract's enforceability. While the snippets provided do not directly discuss conflict of laws or contract interpretation, the LLM explanation states that this undermines established principles of contract interpretation under Maryland law. The added ambiguity concerning fundamental provisions directly challenges the severability clause, a crucial aspect of contract law.
